questions.question: A digital fabrication lab can produce 60 custom circuit boards or 12 high-precision drone frames per week. The lab manager increases drone frame production by 4 units. What is the opportunity cost in circuit boards?
options: 4 circuit boards, matching the increase in drone frame production
20 circuit boards, reflecting the trade-off ratio of five to one
12 circuit boards, equal to the total drone frame production capacity
60 circuit boards, representing the entire circuit board production line
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost of 1 drone frame = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 circuit boards. For 4 additional frames: 4 × 5 = 20 circuit boards sacrificed.

questions.question: Region North produces a ton of wheat in 8 hours and a ton of corn in 2 hours. Region South produces wheat in 10 hours and corn in 5 hours. Which region has the absolute advantage in wheat?
options: Region South, because its corn and wheat production times are closer together
Region North, because it requires fewer labor hours to produce a ton of wheat
Region South, because its opportunity cost of producing wheat is relatively lower
Neither region, because each region leads in a different product's labor time
questions.explanation: Absolute advantage depends on fewer hours per unit. Region North needs 8 hours vs. Region South's 10 hours for a ton of wheat.

questions.question: Country X can produce 400 liters of olive oil or 100 liters of wine. Country Y can produce 300 liters of olive oil or 150 liters of wine. Which country has the comparative advantage in wine?
options: Country X, because it produces more olive oil in absolute terms than Country Y
Country Y, because its opportunity cost of wine is 2 oil versus Country X's 4 oil
Country X, because its wine output per worker exceeds Country Y's wine output
Country Y, because it can produce both olive oil and wine in nearly equal amounts
questions.explanation: Country X's cost of 1 wine = 4 oil (400/100). Country Y's cost = 2 oil (300/150). Country Y has the lower relative cost in wine.

questions.question: A new oil refining technology improves productivity for all goods but helps chemical production more than textile production. How does the PPF shift?
options: The entire PPF shifts outward in a perfectly parallel and uniform manner
An asymmetrical outward shift occurs, favoring the chemical sector's axis more
Only the chemical axis shifts outward while the textile axis remains at its position
The PPF shifts inward for textiles as resources are diverted toward chemicals
questions.explanation: The technology benefits both sectors but chemicals more because it uses oil more intensively. The PPF rotates outward more along the chemical axis.

questions.question: In the Ricardian model under autarky, the unit labor requirement for Good A is 5 and for Good B is 20. What is the autarky price of Good B in terms of Good A?
options: 0.25 units of Good A, calculated as the inverse of the labor requirement ratio
4 units of Good A, equal to the ratio of the two unit labor requirements
5 units of Good A, matching the labor hours needed to produce Good A
20 units of Good A, reflecting the total labor hours for Good B production
questions.explanation: Autarky price ratio = a_LB / a_LA = 20/5 = 4. One unit of Good B costs 4 units of Good A in autarky.

questions.question: The world relative price (P_X/P_Y) falls strictly between the opportunity costs of two trading nations. What is the specialization outcome?
options: Only the country with the lower opportunity cost will choose to specialize
Both countries fully specialize in their respective comparative advantage goods
Neither country specializes because the price benefits neither one fully
Production splits equally between both goods in each of the trading nations
questions.explanation: When the world price is better than each country's domestic cost, both maximize their gains by specializing completely in their comparative advantage good.

questions.question: A large country's labor force grows, expanding its capacity to produce its export good. How does this affect the world relative supply curve?
options: The vertical segment shifts upward as higher costs reduce relative supply
The horizontal step representing that country's output becomes longer
The entire curve shifts to the left as smaller countries lose market share
The curve is unaffected because relative supply depends only on prices
questions.explanation: More labor means the country can supply a larger relative quantity of its export good at its unchanged opportunity cost price.

questions.question: Country A's labor productivity is 3 times higher than its neighbor's in every sector, but Country A's nominal wage is only 2 times higher. What will happen to Country A's trade balance?
options: Country A will import all goods because its wages are absolutely higher
Country A will export all goods until its relative wage is bid upward
Trade will be balanced because the wage ratio matches the productivity ratio
Neither country will trade because absolute advantage determines trade flows
questions.explanation: The productivity lead (3×) exceeds the wage premium (2×), making Country A cheaper in every sector. This creates export surpluses that bid up its relative wage.

questions.question: When moving from autarky to free trade in the Ricardian model, how do real wages change in both trading countries?
options: Real wages fall in both countries because foreign competition reduces domestic prices
Real wages in terms of at least one good must rise in each of the trading countries
Real wages remain unchanged because they are determined solely by domestic MPL
Real wages rise in the exporting country but fall in the importing country
questions.explanation: Trade expands consumption possibilities. Labor in both nations gains purchasing power in terms of at least some goods through cheaper imports.

questions.question: What does the Ricardian model assume about the mobility of labor as a factor of production?
options: Labor is perfectly mobile between countries but immobile between domestic sectors
Labor moves freely between sectors within a country but cannot move across borders
Labor is completely immobile in all dimensions, both domestically and internationally
Labor mobility is determined entirely by government regulation and policy choices
questions.explanation: Perfect domestic labor mobility ensures a single national wage. International immobility preserves wage differences that drive comparative advantage.

questions.question: A country has 1,200 labor hours available, a_LX = 3, and a_LY = 6, with labor as the only factor. What is the equation of its Production Possibility Frontier?
options: 6Q_X + 3Q_Y = 1,200, reflecting the inverse of the labor requirements
3Q_X + 6Q_Y = 1,200, where total labor used equals the labor endowment
Q_X + Q_Y = 1,200, because labor is allocated equally between the two goods
3Q_X – 6Q_Y = 1,200, representing the trade-off between the two outputs
questions.explanation: Total labor constraint: a_LX × Q_X + a_LY × Q_Y = L, so 3Q_X + 6Q_Y = 1,200.

questions.question: A trade policy makes 99% of the population better off but leaves 1% slightly worse off than before. How is this outcome classified in welfare economics?
options: An actual Pareto Improvement because the vast majority of people gain
A potential Pareto Improvement, not an actual one, because some are worse off
A Kaldor-Hicks deterioration because the minority is harmed by the policy
Neither an improvement nor a deterioration because the effects cancel out
questions.explanation: It is not an actual Pareto Improvement because at least one person loses. The 99% could theoretically compensate the 1%, making it a potential Pareto Improvement.

questions.question: An economy is producing at a point located inside its Production Possibility Frontier. Is this allocation Pareto Efficient?
options: Yes, because the economy is not exceeding its resource constraints
No, because more of one good could be produced without sacrificing the other
Yes, because all points on or inside the PPF are equally efficient
No, but only if the economy is also experiencing high inflation
questions.explanation: Pareto Efficiency requires being on the PPF boundary. Inside the frontier, output of at least one good can increase without reducing the other.

questions.question: The price of wheat is \$15 and a_LW = 3. The price of corn is \$10 and a_LC = 1. What will a worker earn per hour producing wheat in the Ricardian model?
options: \$15 per hour, matching the total market price of a unit of wheat
\$5 per hour, calculated as the price divided by the unit labor requirement
\$3 per hour, equal to the unit labor requirement for wheat production
\$45 per hour, reflecting the product of price and labor requirement
questions.explanation: Wage in a sector = P ÷ a_Li. Wheat wage = \$15 ÷ 3 = \$5 per hour.

questions.question: In the Ricardian model, the wage in clothing is \$20 and the wage in electronics is \$18. Labor is perfectly mobile. What will happen?
options: Wages will equalize at \$19 as workers split evenly between sectors
All workers will migrate from electronics into the clothing sector
Workers will remain in their current sectors because the gap is small
The government will intervene to set a uniform wage of \$20 for all
questions.explanation: With perfect mobility and constant MPL, labor shifts entirely to the higher-wage clothing sector until electronics production ceases.

questions.question: Why is the opportunity cost of Good X in terms of Good Y constant in the Ricardian model?
options: Because the government fixes production levels and prevents reallocation
Labor is the only input and exhibits constant returns to scale in both sectors
Diminishing returns ensure that each additional worker produces the same
The model assumes that capital and labor can be freely substituted for each other
questions.explanation: Unit labor requirements are fixed technological parameters. The ratio a_LX / a_LY never changes regardless of how much of each good is produced.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, trade causes the price of an imported good to fall. What happens to the return on specialized machinery used to produce that good domestically?
options: The return rises because lower prices stimulate higher consumer demand
The return on the machinery falls significantly because it cannot be relocated
The return remains stable because machinery values are set by replacement cost
The return shifts to other sectors as the machinery is quickly repurposed
questions.explanation: The specialized machine cannot move to another sector. Its value and return are tied strictly to the declining price of its specific output.

questions.question: Why does the immobile factor model predict stronger political opposition to trade than the Ricardian model?
options: Because trade benefits are larger in the immobile model, creating more conflict
It identifies specific groups trapped in declining sectors with no exit option
The Ricardian model assumes all workers lose from trade in every scenario
Immobile factor models predict no net gains from trade for the nation overall
questions.explanation: In the Ricardian model, workers can switch sectors. With immobile factors, losses are concentrated on trapped groups, generating intense political resistance.

questions.question: A nation opens to trade and the price of its export good rises by 15% in the immobile factor model. What happens to the nominal income of workers specific to the export sector?
options: Nominal income rises by less than 15% because some gains go to consumers
Nominal income increases by exactly 15%, moving with the export price one-for-one
Nominal income remains unchanged because wages are set by long-term contracts
Nominal income rises by more than 15% due to the Magnification Effect on wages
questions.explanation: With immobile labor, nominal wages in the export sector are tied directly to the price of the good produced. A 15% price increase raises nominal income by 15%.

questions.question: Country A has a K/L ratio of 2. Country B has a K/L ratio of 5. Good X uses 1K and 1L. Good Y uses 3K and 1L. Which good will Country B export according to the H-O model?
options: Good X, because it uses fewer total units of capital and labor to produce
Good Y, because Country B is capital-abundant and Good Y is capital-intensive
Good X, because Country B's higher ratio makes simple goods more profitable
Both goods equally, because factor abundance does not determine trade patterns
questions.explanation: Country B is capital-abundant (K/L = 5 > 2). Good Y is capital-intensive (K/L = 3/1 > 1/1). Capital-abundant countries export capital-intensive goods.

questions.question: According to the Heckscher-Ohlin model, what happens to the real income of the scarce factor when a country opens to international trade?
options: Real income increases because trade expands all sectors of the economy
Real income decreases, as predicted by the Stolper-Samuelson theorem
Real income remains constant because factor endowments are fixed
Real income rises for labor but falls for capital regardless of abundance
questions.explanation: The Stolper-Samuelson theorem states that trade reduces the real return to the economy's relatively scarce factor of production.

questions.question: The price of the labor-intensive good rises. According to the Stolper-Samuelson mechanism, how does the capital-to-labor ratio change in both industries?
options: The K/L ratio falls in both sectors as firms hire more workers to expand
The K/L ratio rises in both sectors as wages increase relative to capital costs
The K/L ratio rises in labor-intensive sectors but falls in capital-intensive ones
The K/L ratio remains unchanged because factor intensity is technologically fixed
questions.explanation: Higher wages relative to capital rents (w/r rises) cause firms in both industries to substitute capital for the now more expensive labor.

questions.question: Which condition is necessary for the Factor-Price Equalization theorem to hold perfectly in the real world?
options: One country must be significantly larger than the other in economic size
Countries must use identical technologies and have no barriers to trade
Labor must be perfectly mobile across international borders at all times
Both countries must have exactly the same capital-to-labor endowment ratio
questions.explanation: FPE requires the Law of One Price for goods and identical production functions across countries. Technology differences or trade barriers prevent full equalization.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, both P_F and P_M increase by exactly 10%. What happens to the nominal wage in the economy?
options: The nominal wage remains unchanged because real variables stay constant
The nominal wage increases by exactly 10%, matching the uniform price rise
The nominal wage rises by more than 10% due to the Magnification Effect
The nominal wage rises by less than 10% because labor is only partially mobile
questions.explanation: If all prices rise by the same percentage, labor demand curves shift up proportionally. The nominal wage rises by 10% while real variables remain unchanged.

questions.question: The supply of capital specific to Manufacturing increases in the Specific Factor Model. How are Landowners in Agriculture affected?
options: Their real return rises because a stronger manufacturing sector helps everyone
Their real return decreases as higher wages pull labor away from farming
Their real return remains constant because land and factories are separate
Their nominal return rises but real return falls, leaving the net effect zero
questions.explanation: More manufacturing capital raises wages, drawing mobile labor out of agriculture and reducing the marginal productivity and rental return of land.

questions.question: How is the Value of the Marginal Product of Labor defined in the Specific Factor Model?
options: The marginal product of labor divided by the market price of the good
The market price of the good multiplied by the marginal product of labor
The total output of the sector divided by the number of workers employed
The wage rate minus the rental cost of the specific factor used in production
questions.explanation: VMPL = P × MPL. This curve represents the labor demand for each sector, showing how much firms are willing to pay for an additional worker.

questions.question: Labor is the mobile factor in the Specific Factor Model. How is the total labor force allocated between sectors in equilibrium?
options: Equally, with half of all workers in manufacturing and half in agriculture
So that L_M + L_A = L and P_M × MPL_M = P_A × MPL_A across both sectors
Based on government mandates that set employment quotas for each industry
Entirely into whichever sector has the higher absolute price for its output
questions.explanation: Labor moves until the nominal wage (VMPL) is equalized across both sectors, eliminating any incentive for further movement.

questions.question: In the Krugman model, how does the variety benefit of trade affect consumer welfare even if total consumption quantity stays the same?
options: Variety has no effect on welfare because only quantity matters to consumers
Consumer utility increases because people value having more choices available
Variety reduces welfare by confusing consumers with too many product options
The variety benefit applies only to producers, not to consumers in the model
questions.explanation: Consumers generally prefer more choices. Trade combines the product varieties of two previously separate markets, increasing total available variety and utility.

questions.question: An industry experiences external economies of scale, and several new firms enter the geographic cluster. What happens to average costs for all firms?
options: Average costs rise because more firms compete for the same limited resources
Average costs decrease for all firms as the larger industry improves efficiency
Average costs are unaffected because each firm's costs depend only on its own size
Average costs rise for incumbents but fall for the new entrants joining the cluster
questions.explanation: External economies benefit the entire industry. A larger cluster provides better infrastructure, specialized labor pools, and knowledge spillovers for all firms.

questions.question: What does the product cycle theory predict about the pattern of international trade over time?
options: Products are always exported by the country that first invented them permanently
New products are first exported by high-tech nations and later by low-cost nations as they mature
Trade in agricultural products follows a different cycle than trade in manufactured goods
The product life cycle has no relationship to international trade patterns at all
questions.explanation: The product cycle theory links trade to technological life cycles. Innovative nations export first; as products standardize, production shifts to lower-cost countries.

questions.question: Under what condition can the Effective Rate of Protection for a domestic industry become negative?
options: When the nominal tariff on the final product is set at exactly zero percent
When tariffs on imported inputs are significantly higher than the tariff on the final good
When the domestic industry exports more than it sells in the home market
Effective rates of protection can never be negative by mathematical definition
questions.explanation: High input tariffs tax domestic producers more than the output tariff protects them, potentially making the net effective protection negative.

questions.question: What is the net effect of a tariff on national welfare when imposed by a small country?
options: Welfare increases because the tariff protects domestic jobs and industry
National welfare decreases due to deadweight losses with no terms-of-trade gain
Welfare remains unchanged because small countries cannot affect world prices
Welfare increases for producers but the net national effect is always zero
questions.explanation: A small country cannot improve its terms of trade. The tariff creates production and consumption distortions that reduce national welfare.

questions.question: What is trade diversion in the context of forming a Customs Union?
options: Creating new trade flows that did not exist before the union was formed
Shifting imports from an efficient non-member to a less efficient member country
Redirecting exports from one member country toward another member country
The relocation of factories to member countries with the lowest tax rates
questions.explanation: Trade diversion is welfare-reducing. Preferential tariff removal causes imports to shift from the globally most efficient producer to a higher-cost bloc member.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of imposing anti-dumping duties on imported goods?
options: To raise general government revenue from all categories of imported merchandise
To offset the price advantage created when foreign firms sell below cost or home-market price
To encourage domestic firms to export more by matching the foreign competitors' prices
To comply with WTO rules that require all countries to impose some anti-dumping measures
questions.explanation: Anti-dumping duties neutralize the unfair price advantage created when foreign firms engage in dumping, selling exports at artificially low prices.

questions.question: A large country imposes a tariff. The world price falls by \$10 and the domestic price rises by \$15. How should the deadweight loss be understood?
options: The loss equals \$10, matching the world price decrease from the tariff
The loss arises from the \$25 total tariff, partly offset by the \$10 terms-of-trade gain
The loss equals \$15, reflecting only the domestic price increase component
There is no deadweight loss because the terms-of-trade gain exceeds the distortion
questions.explanation: Total tariff = \$15 + \$10 = \$25. The country gains \$10 per unit from improved terms of trade but loses from the domestic distortions on the full \$25 tariff.

questions.question: Which of the following is the best example of a compound tariff on an imported product?
options: A 15% tax calculated solely on the assessed customs value of the good
A duty of \$0.50 per pound plus an additional 10% of the good's total value
A tariff that increases from 5% to 15% when imports exceed a threshold
A tax applied only when the good originates from certain named countries
questions.explanation: A compound tariff combines a specific (per-unit) charge with an ad valorem (percentage of value) component on the same imported good.

questions.question: How does the imposition of a tariff affect domestic producer surplus in the importing country?
options: Producer surplus decreases because the tariff raises input costs for firms
Producer surplus increases as domestic firms sell more at higher protected prices
Producer surplus remains constant because it is determined by world prices
Producer surplus initially falls but recovers as foreign competitors exit the market
questions.explanation: Domestic producers receive a higher price for their output and expand production along their supply curve, increasing their producer surplus.

questions.question: A large country applies its optimal tariff rate. What economic outcome is being maximized?
options: Government tariff revenue collected from all categories of imported goods
National welfare, balancing the terms-of-trade gain against the deadweight loss
The volume of domestic production in the import-competing sector of the economy
Consumer surplus, ensuring the lowest possible prices for domestic buyers
questions.explanation: The optimal tariff is the rate where the marginal terms-of-trade gain equals the marginal efficiency loss from domestic distortions, maximizing national welfare.

questions.question: What defines a global quota as a trade policy instrument?
options: A quota negotiated through the WTO that applies to all member countries equally
A limit on total imports of a good regardless of which country supplies them
A quota that applies to every product category imported by a country
A restriction that targets imports only from the largest supplying countries
questions.explanation: A global quota sets one total ceiling on imports of a product from all countries combined, without discriminating between different supplying nations.

questions.question: Why does a quota system generate rent-seeking behavior among domestic firms?
options: Quotas require firms to pay taxes on every unit they import into the country
The right to import under a quota is valuable, so firms spend resources to obtain licenses
Quota rents are automatically distributed by the government to the most efficient firms
Rent-seeking only occurs under tariffs, not under any form of import quota system
questions.explanation: The quota rent is the profit from buying at the low world price and selling at the high domestic price. Firms waste resources lobbying to capture this rent.

questions.question: What does the Most-Favored Nation principle require of World Trade Organization members?
options: Granting special tariff preferences to the country's largest trading partner
Applying the same tariff rates uniformly to all other WTO member countries
Providing foreign aid to the least developed member countries of the WTO
Allowing free immigration of workers from all other WTO member nations
questions.explanation: The MFN principle prevents discriminatory trade practices by requiring each member to treat all other WTO members equally in trade policy.

questions.question: How can Sanitary and Phytosanitary measures function as barriers to international trade?
options: By directly taxing agricultural imports at rates higher than industrial goods
By setting health standards that are uniquely difficult for foreign producers to satisfy
By establishing numerical quotas on all food products entering the country
By subsidizing domestic farmers to meet all international safety requirements
questions.explanation: While technically for health and safety, SPS measures can be designed to favor domestic producers and create costly hurdles for foreign agricultural exporters.

questions.question: A country exports \$600 billion and imports \$750 billion in goods, with net investment income of +\$50 billion. What is the balance on goods and services?
options: +\$100 billion, combining the trade balance with the net investment income
–\$150 billion, calculated as the difference between exports and imports of goods
–\$100 billion, netting the trade deficit against the positive investment income
+\$50 billion, reflecting only the net investment income from abroad
questions.explanation: Balance on goods and services = Exports – Imports = \$600B – \$750B = –\$150B. Investment income is part of the Current Account but not the trade balance.

questions.question: A foreign company earns profit in the host country and reinvests it into its local subsidiary. How is this recorded in the host country's Balance of Payments?
options: As a credit in both the Primary Income and Financial Account simultaneously
As a debit in Primary Income and a credit in the Financial Account as FDI inflow
As a debit in the Current Account and a debit in the Official Reserves Account
The transaction is not recorded because the money never leaves the host country
questions.explanation: The profit earned is an outflow (debit in Primary Income). Reinvesting it into the local subsidiary is a capital inflow (credit in the Financial Account as FDI).

questions.question: What purpose does the Statistical Discrepancy serve in the Balance of Payments accounting framework?
options: It measures the difference between a country's nominal and real exchange rates
An adjusting entry that forces the sum of all BoP accounts to equal exactly zero
It tracks the change in the central bank's official foreign exchange reserves
It records transactions that are too small to classify into other BoP categories
questions.explanation: Because BoP data comes from many different sources, errors and omissions are inevitable. The statistical discrepancy is the balancing plug figure.

questions.question: A country's national saving is negative (S < 0). What must be true about its Current Account balance?
options: The Current Account must be in surplus to offset the negative saving
The Current Account must be in deficit to satisfy the identity S = I + CA
The Current Account is unaffected because saving and trade are independent
The Current Account equals exactly zero when national saving is negative
questions.explanation: From S = I + CA, if S is negative and I is positive or zero, then CA must be negative (a deficit) for the identity to hold.

questions.question: A Big Mac costs \$6 in the US and 500 Yen in Japan. The exchange rate is 100 Yen per Dollar. What does Purchasing Power Parity suggest about the Japanese Yen?
options: The Yen is overvalued because 500 Yen is more than the Dollar equivalent
The Yen is undervalued relative to the PPP-implied rate of 83.3 Yen per Dollar
The exchange rate is exactly at PPP equilibrium because both are major currencies
PPP cannot make predictions about currencies using only a single product's price
questions.explanation: Implied PPP rate = 500 Yen ÷ \$6 = 83.3 Yen/$. The actual rate is 100 Yen/$, meaning the Dollar is stronger and the Yen is weaker (undervalued) than PPP predicts.

questions.question: What does sterilization refer to when a central bank intervenes in the foreign exchange market?
options: Eliminating all foreign currency reserves to allow the currency to float freely
Offsetting the money supply impact of intervention through domestic open-market operations
Permanently fixing the exchange rate so it can never be adjusted in the future
Banning all private foreign exchange transactions to prevent currency speculation
questions.explanation: If the central bank buys foreign currency (increasing the money supply), it may simultaneously sell domestic bonds to absorb that liquidity and keep the money supply stable.

questions.question: What characterizes a managed float exchange rate system?
options: The exchange rate is permanently fixed by law and never changes at all
The rate is set by market forces but the central bank intervenes to smooth volatility
A committee of commercial bank CEOs meets weekly to set the official rate
Multiple countries share a single currency managed by a supranational central bank
questions.explanation: A managed float is a hybrid system. The exchange rate is primarily market-determined, but the central bank intervenes periodically to prevent excessive fluctuations.

questions.question: UK interest rate is 4%, US interest rate is 6%, and the spot rate is \$1.25 per Pound. What is the approximate one-year forward rate according to Covered Interest Parity?
options: \$1.225 per Pound, reflecting the Pound's expected depreciation over the year
\$1.275 per Pound, calculated as the spot rate times the interest rate ratio
\$1.250 per Pound, because forward rates always equal the current spot rate
\$1.300 per Pound, reflecting the higher US interest rate relative to the UK
